title: A place that looks ordinary but matters to you
summary: Describe an ordinary place that matters to you, narrate one specific moment there, and explain why it has stayed with you.
topic: A place that looks ordinary but matters to you
prompt: Write 3 paragraphs (350–500 words) about an ordinary place that matters to you. Describe it, narrate one moment there, and explain why it matters. Use relative clauses, present perfect, and modals.
hintStarters: There is a place I have returned to so many times that I know exactly how it…
The day I remember most clearly, I was sitting there when…
Although it would mean nothing to a stranger, this place has become…

hintVocab.meaningVi: hòa lẫn vào nền, không gây chú ý
nhìn ra một nơi / bỏ qua một nơi
cảm thấy thoải mái, như ở nhà
tìm kiếm, tìm đến một nơi
neo đậu bạn, giữ bạn lại

sampleText:
There is a small stone pier at the edge of the old harbour in Lisbon where I used to go every evening for almost a year. It is the kind of place that most tourists walk past without stopping: no sign, no café, no view that photographs well. The pier is short, barely long enough for six people to stand on, and the stone is uneven underfoot from decades of salt water and traffic. A rusted iron ring, which was probably used to tie boats, is bolted into the side near the water. To anyone who does not know its history, it looks like nothing worth noticing.

I discovered it by accident during a period when I had just arrived in the city and was finding it difficult to settle. One afternoon, I was walking along the waterfront when the noise of the main street faded behind me and I found myself standing on the pier. I sat down, looked out at the river, and lost track of time for the first time in months. After that, I went back whenever I needed to think clearly. I have sat there in heat and in rain, in the early morning before the city woke up, and late at night when the water was the colour of oil. Each time, I left feeling more settled than when I arrived.

Although I have moved to a different city now, I still think about that pier more often than I can easily explain. There are places which hold something of the person you were when you found them. That pier would mean nothing to a stranger who walked out onto it today, but for me it marks the year when I had to decide what kind of life I was going to build. It did not give me any answers. But it was the kind of place where the questions felt manageable, and sometimes that is all you really need. I should go back one day — though part of me worries it will have changed, and the version I carry in my memory might be the one that matters most.

một vòng sắt đã han gỉ
tình cờ khám phá ra nó

sampleAnnotations.phrase: the noise of the main street faded behind me
sampleAnnotations.meaningVi: tiếng ồn của phố chính tan biến phía sau tôi

sampleAnnotations.phrase: whenever I needed to think clearly
sampleAnnotations.meaningVi: bất cứ khi nào tôi cần suy nghĩ rõ ràng

sampleAnnotations.phrase: the water was the colour of oil

sampleAnnotations.meaningVi: rời đi cảm thấy ổn định hơn

sampleAnnotations.phrase: hold something of the person you were
sampleAnnotations.meaningVi: lưu giữ điều gì đó của con người bạn khi đó

sampleAnnotations.phrase: marks the year when I had to decide
sampleAnnotations.meaningVi: đánh dấu năm khi tôi phải quyết định
những câu hỏi cảm thấy có thể xử lý được

sampleAnnotations.phrase: the version I carry in my memory
sampleAnnotations.meaningVi: phiên bản tôi mang trong ký ức

sampleAnnotations.phrase: barely long enough for six people to stand on
sampleAnnotations.meaningVi: vừa đủ chỗ cho sáu người đứng

sampleGrammarNotes.title: Relative clauses to add description without a new sentence
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: 'A rusted iron ring, which was probably used to tie boats, is bolted into the side near the water.' Mệnh đề quan hệ (relative clause) với 'which' bổ sung thông tin về danh từ phía trước mà không cần câu mới. Khi mệnh đề quan hệ không thiết yếu (non-defining), nó được đặt trong dấu phẩy.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: 'A rusted iron ring, which was probably used to tie boats, is bolted into the side near the water.' A relative clause with 'which' adds information about the noun before it without starting a new sentence. When the relative clause is non-defining (extra information), it is separated by commas.

sampleGrammarNotes.title: Present perfect with 'have + past participle' for accumulated experience
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: 'I have sat there in heat and in rain…' dùng hiện tại hoàn thành để liệt kê các lần đến thăm đã tích lũy theo thời gian, không gắn với một thời điểm cụ thể. 'I have moved to a different city now' cho thấy kết quả của một hành động trong quá khứ vẫn còn đúng ở hiện tại.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: 'I have sat there in heat and in rain…' uses the present perfect to list accumulated visits over time, not tied to one specific date. 'I have moved to a different city now' shows the result of a past action that is still true in the present.

sampleTranslationVi:
Có một cầu tàu đá nhỏ ở rìa bến cảng cũ ở Lisbon nơi tôi hay đến mỗi tối gần một năm. Đây là loại nơi mà hầu hết khách du lịch đi qua mà không dừng lại: không có biển hiệu, không có quán cà phê, không có tầm nhìn nào chụp ảnh đẹp. Cầu tàu rất ngắn, vừa đủ chỗ cho sáu người đứng, và mặt đá gồ ghề dưới chân vì hàng thập kỷ nước muối và qua lại. Một vòng sắt đã han gỉ, có lẽ từng được dùng để buộc thuyền, được bắt vít vào bên cạnh gần mặt nước. Với bất kỳ ai không biết lịch sử của nó, nó trông chẳng có gì đáng để chú ý.

Tôi tình cờ khám phá ra nó trong một giai đoạn khi tôi vừa đến thành phố và đang thấy khó ổn định. Một buổi chiều, tôi đang đi dọc bờ sông thì tiếng ồn của phố chính tan biến phía sau tôi và tôi thấy mình đang đứng trên cầu tàu. Tôi ngồi xuống, nhìn ra dòng sông, và quên mất thời gian lần đầu tiên trong nhiều tháng. Sau đó, tôi quay lại bất cứ khi nào tôi cần suy nghĩ rõ ràng. Tôi đã ngồi ở đó trong nắng và trong mưa, vào buổi sáng sớm trước khi thành phố thức dậy, và khuya tối khi mặt nước có màu dầu. Mỗi lần, tôi rời đi cảm thấy ổn định hơn so với khi đến.

Mặc dù tôi đã chuyển đến một thành phố khác rồi, tôi vẫn nghĩ về cái cầu tàu đó thường xuyên hơn tôi có thể dễ dàng giải thích. Có những nơi lưu giữ điều gì đó của con người bạn khi bạn tìm thấy chúng. Cái cầu tàu đó sẽ chẳng có ý nghĩa gì với người lạ bước ra đó hôm nay, nhưng với tôi nó đánh dấu năm khi tôi phải quyết định mình sẽ xây dựng cuộc sống kiểu gì. Nó không cho tôi câu trả lời nào. Nhưng đây là loại nơi mà những câu hỏi cảm thấy có thể xử lý được, và đôi khi đó là tất cả những gì bạn thực sự cần. Tôi nên quay lại một ngày nào đó — dù một phần trong tôi lo lắng rằng nó sẽ đã thay đổi, và phiên bản tôi mang trong ký ức có thể là phiên bản quan trọng nhất.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses a non-defining relative clause correctly?
options: A rusted iron ring which was probably used to tie boats is bolted into the side.
A rusted iron ring, that was probably used to tie boats, is bolted into the side.
A rusted iron ring, which was probably used to tie boats, is bolted into the side.
A rusted iron ring, which was probably used to tie boats is bolted into the side.
mcQuestions.explanation: Non-defining relative clauses (extra, non-essential information) use 'which' (not 'that') and are surrounded by commas. Both commas are required.
mcQuestions.hint: Non-defining relative clauses need commas on both sides; 'that' cannot be used here.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses the present perfect correctly to express accumulated experience?
options: I sat there in heat and in rain, in the early morning and late at night.
I have been sitting there in heat and in rain in the early morning and late at night.
I have sit there in heat and in rain, in the early morning and late at night.
I have sat there in heat and in rain, in the early morning and late at night.
mcQuestions.explanation: Present perfect ('have sat') expresses accumulated, repeated experience over time without specifying exact dates. 'Have sit' is wrong — 'sit' has an irregular past participle: 'sat'.
mcQuestions.hint: The irregular past participle of 'sit' is 'sat'; use present perfect for repeated past experience.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence correctly uses 'although' for concession before a main claim?
options: Although I have moved to a different city now, I still think about that pier often.
Although I have moved to a different city now, but I still think about that pier often.
I have moved to a different city now, although I still think about that pier often.
Although, I have moved to a different city now I still think about that pier often.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Although + subordinate clause, main clause' — no 'but' in the main clause, and no comma directly after 'although'. The concessive clause must come before the main clause for B1 essay style.
mcQuestions.hint: 'Although' introduces the subordinate clause first, with no 'but' in the main clause.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence correctly uses 'should' to express intention or mild obligation?
options: I should to go back one day, though part of me worries it will have changed.
I should go back one day, though part of me worries it will have changed.
I should going back one day, though part of me worries it will have changed.
I should went back one day, though part of me worries it will have changed.
mcQuestions.explanation: Modal verbs like 'should' are followed by the bare infinitive (base form). Never 'to + infinitive' or a participle.
mcQuestions.hint: 'Should' + bare infinitive; no 'to' between the modal and the verb.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which defining relative clause correctly identifies a type of place?
options: There are places which they hold something of the person you were when you found them.
There are places which hold something of the person you were when you found it.
There are places which hold something of the person you were when you found them.
There are places what hold something of the person you were when you found them.
mcQuestions.explanation: After a relative pronoun, do not repeat the subject with 'they'. 'Which' is used for things; 'what' cannot introduce a relative clause after a noun.
mcQuestions.hint: After 'which', do not add 'they'; 'what' cannot follow a noun in a relative clause.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses the past continuous correctly to describe a background action?
options: I walked along the waterfront when the noise was fading behind me.
I was walking along the waterfront when the noise has faded behind me.
I am walking along the waterfront when the noise faded behind me.
I was walking along the waterfront when the noise faded behind me.
mcQuestions.explanation: Past continuous + past simple: the past continuous describes a longer background action ('was walking'); the past simple describes the shorter event that interrupted it ('faded').
mcQuestions.hint: Use past continuous for the action in progress, and past simple for the interrupting event.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which phrase is the correct collocation for losing awareness of time?
options: lost track of time
lost trace of time
lost count of time
lost sight of time
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Lose track of time' is the fixed idiom meaning you stop noticing how much time has passed. 'Lose trace/count/sight of time' are not standard collocations in this sense.
mcQuestions.hint: This is a fixed idiom: 'lose ___ of time' — which noun completes it?

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses 'whenever' correctly to express a repeated conditional?
options: I went back whenever I need to think clearly.
I went back whenever I needed to think clearly.
I went back whenever I was needing to think clearly.
I went back whenever I have needed to think clearly.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Whenever + past simple' in a narrative past context describes a repeated condition in the past. The main clause is also in the past simple.
mcQuestions.hint: When the main clause is past, the 'whenever' clause is also past simple.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence correctly describes a result using 'left + adjective'?
options: Each time, I left to feel more settled than when I arrived.
Each time, I left and felt more settled than when I arrived.
Each time, I left feeling more settled than when I arrived.
Each time, I have left feeling more settled than when I arrived.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Left feeling' uses a present participle to describe your state at the moment of leaving. 'Left to feel' would suggest purpose; 'left and felt' is grammatically fine but sounds less natural and fluent at B1.
mcQuestions.hint: 'Left feeling + adjective' describes your state as you departed — the participle phrase is the most natural structure.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence correctly uses the present perfect passive to describe a change to a place?
options: The pier has renovated since I last visited.
The pier was renovated since I last visited.
The pier have been renovated since I last visited.
The pier has been renovated since I last visited.
mcQuestions.explanation: Present perfect passive = 'has/have been + past participle'. 'The pier' is singular, so 'has' is correct. 'Was renovated' + 'since' is wrong because 'since' requires perfect tenses.
mcQuestions.hint: Present perfect passive: 'has been + past participle'; use 'since' with present perfect, not past simple.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which option correctly uses a modal to express that something might still be true?
options: The version I carry in my memory might be the one that matters most.
The version I carry in my memory might to be the one that matters most.
The version I carry in my memory might being the one that matters most.
The version I carry in my memory mights be the one that matters most.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Might' + bare infinitive expresses possibility. Modals never take 'to' before the infinitive, and 'mights' does not exist — modals have no third-person -s.
mcQuestions.hint: Modals like 'might' are always followed by the bare infinitive; they never add -s.

criticalThinkingQuestion: Can a place matter to you if it no longer exists in the same form? Or does the meaning only live in the physical place itself — and what does your answer say about the nature of memory?
